content: Hey, I just with the kernel and was trying to find a bug to fix and I couldn't I surfed through 5 pages of issues but most have merged pr's or open pr's from 2+ years ago that seem to be stale. I understand most projects don't want to close pr's just because of age, but at a point of size in the project it becomes unmanageable. I can't find a nice starter task within the issue list. Any suggestions on anything that needs minor changes or fixed?
Most activity happens on the mailing list these days, rather than on GitHub.

Alice Ryhl said:

Most activity happens on the mailing list these days, rather than on GitHub.

do you have any suggestions on what I should start with then?
You can check-in with Kartik1397 about this. He showed interest in early December but I can't find a PR or mailing list patch.

@Charalampos Mitrodimas Anything else that comes to mind? I am facing the same issues as @Carson Range, that there is no good place for people to get started and fix small issues or to know what to work on.
I agree, a pr for that issue is awaiting approval

There are three things that I noticed while reading through the kernel crate but did not include in my PR since I think they require some further discussion with developers and maintainers:

  • The inner field of UniqueArc is private, but it is mentioned in the Invariants section of its docs, which is public. Similar issues might exist in other places. (The invariants section is here)
  • Oftentimes trait/struct/enum name::function/method name syntax is used to refer to functions/methods of the type that is being documented. This might be unnecessarily verbose. (Example: ForeignOwnable).
  • Oftentimes doclinks link back to the item that is being documented (Example: Arc, ARef, ...). This might be a bit redundant. The standard library has a paragraph in their coding/contributing guidelines with advises against that.

All of those things are a bit opinionated and should probably be discussed here before starting to work on it. Hope this helps you :)

Valentin Obst said:

All of those things are a bit opinionated and should probably be discussed here before starting to work on it. Hope this helps you :)

It does, but the when I search todo's through the the rust folder I only get 3. Which is because we don't have a c macro, which isn't that important. Are there other features of the kernel you guys would like work to be started on? since it seems like everything within RFL is complete and refined.

From reading the RFL mailing list, I saw two things that have been requested:

  • A demo implementation of ext2 filesystem to see if the fs abstractions being developed are sound
  • A rewrite of the nouveau driver

I don't know if anyone is working on any of these two, but if not these two tasks would be doable with commonly available hardware.

From reading the RFL mailing list, I saw two things that have been requested:

  • A rewrite of the nouveau driver

I don't know if anyone is working on any of these two, but if not these two tasks would be doable with commonly available hardware.

I haven't checked for something else.

One idea that I had in mind in terms of abstractions was for power_supply. But this is a bigger task and probably not suitable for beginners (at least the first part, the abstractions itself, not the a driver). Although I'm not sure if it's something we'd need for rust-for-linux.

Cc. @Miguel Ojeda

Do you know where the ext2 filesystem struct is? I can't find it in include/linux/ext2_fs.h, and I don't know anything about power supplys. For filesystems I at least understand the basics, enough to be helpful.

You can use external tools like https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/latest/source for this.

The inner field of UniqueArc is private, but it is mentioned in the Invariants section of its docs, which is public. Similar issues might exist in other places. (The invariants section is here)

Note that ext2 would require the filesystem abstractions that @Wedson Almeida Filho is working on (I think he may also want to send ext2 or perhaps another FS as an example for the abstractions).

Charalampos Mitrodimas said:

One idea that I had in mind in terms of abstractions was for power_supply. But this is a bigger task and probably not suitable for beginners (at least the first part, the abstractions itself, not the a driver). Although I'm not sure if it's something we'd need for rust-for-linux.

Cc. Miguel Ojeda

Work is normally spearheaded by the users (e.g. drivers) that want to be upstreamed. In other words, normally you need an expected user in order to be able to upstream abstractions for a given subsystem, plus agreement from their maintainers. Please see https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing#ways-to-contribute and https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing#submitting-new-abstractions-and-modules -- we have some guidelines / general advice there.
I think @Ariel Miculas mentioned working on ext2, but not sure whether that happened
Also @Shashwat Kishore mentioned that Microchip is looking for phy drivers for the VSC825{4,6,7,8} if that interests anyone.
Shashwat is working on the VSC84XX but apparently getting hardware for these phys is somewhat of a struggle

Good to see if Ariel or Wedson have anything done

Should I reach out over email, or wait here if they check Zulip?

sounds great!

Here are my latest changes: https://github.com/wedsonaf/linux/commits/ext2fs/

I did what upstream requested in terms of splitting up the operations, plus added iomap support for ro filesystems, and incorporated feedback.

It also has a ro implementation of ext2. I'll add write support in the coming days and send it to the mailing list for review.

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

Here are my latest changes: https://github.com/wedsonaf/linux/commits/ext2fs/

I did what upstream requested in terms of splitting up the operations, plus added iomap support for ro filesystems, and incorporated feedback.

It also has a ro implementation of ext2. I'll add write support in the coming days and send it to the mailing list for review.

is there anything I can help with or is this covered by you?

Here's one area that we need improvement: making owned C strings "foreign ownable". There are two cases where the C side has a single pointer and we would like to store a CString there; the problem is that the current implementation uses a Vec, which has 3 words in it: pointer, length, and capacity.

I have a patch that changes it to use a Box<[u8]> instead, and I implemented ForeignOwnable by storing just a pointer on the C side. Then to reconstruct the CString, I calculate the length by searching for the null terminator (which is a type invariant). This works ok, but makes borrowing or converting back to CString an O(n) operation. Ideally, we'd like it to be O(1).

Miguel suggested that we use ThinBox instead, but it needs some functions (e.g., from_raw/into_raw). The upside is that we could get O(1) behaviour storing just a pointer on the C side.

Another challange we have with this is having to pre-initialise the string to avoid unsafe blocks. Here's the actual scenario: we need to allocate the buffer then populate it from block storage (different parts may come from different blocks, so the source string is not all available in one shot), then actually convert to CString (e.g., ensure that the string is null-terminated and doesn't contain null elsewhere). If we use [MaybeUninit<u8>], we need an unsafe block to convert it to [u8], which we want to avoid. Otherwise, we need to fill the slice with some value to ensure it's initialised (this is what we do), but it's wasteful because we are soon going to overwrite it with something else.

Anyway, is this something that may interest you?

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

Anyway, is this something that may interest you?

Yeah I could take this on, should i clone your fork of rfl?
You can probably start from rust-next or master, I wouldn't expect that to have fs dependencies (and systems outside of fs can probably put it to use)

You can get the following commits from my fork:

85eaa9c042c4 rust: alloc: introduce `try_new_slice` to `Box<[T]>`
e44a8cc602dc rust: str: add conversion from `&[u8]` to `CString`
cf176f1f93c6 rust: str: implement `TryFrom<Box<[u8]>>` for CString
b444a3e0c544 rust: str: implement `ForeignOwnable` for `CString`
eb20ca33b9e9 rust: str: modify `CString` to use `Box<[u8]>` instead of `Vec`

And use them as a starting point in rust-next.

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

You can get the following commits from my fork:

85eaa9c042c4 rust: alloc: introduce `try_new_slice` to `Box<[T]>`
e44a8cc602dc rust: str: add conversion from `&[u8]` to `CString`
cf176f1f93c6 rust: str: implement `TryFrom<Box<[u8]>>` for CString
b444a3e0c544 rust: str: implement `ForeignOwnable` for `CString`
eb20ca33b9e9 rust: str: modify `CString` to use `Box<[u8]>` instead of `Vec`

And use them as a starting point in rust-next.

wdym folling commits? is that like a fork?

I meant to say that these commits implementation the functionality I mentioned before (O(n) ForeignOwnable for CString +
initialisation with the extra perf cost of initialising each byte). You can refer to/use them if that'll help you implement the better version.

But the better version should be based directly on rust-next

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

But the better version should be based directly on rust-next

So just work directly on my own fork and then draft a pr for you to reivew? and what file is this change needed in.

Yes, draft a pr and we'll review it.

We're looking for an efficient implementation of ForeignOwnable for CString. So you probably want to begin in rust/kernel/str.rs but you may need changes elsewhere, as exemplified by the commits I listed above.
To be clear, what is being requested here is not trivial; if you're a real beginner, you probably want to start with something simpler

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

To be clear, what is being requested here is not trivial; if you're a real beginner, you probably want to start with something simpler

I'm just a beginner to the kernel, how to guys use git is something I've been stuggling with but I can handle the coding, but it doesn't look like I have those commits in my tree. 

─redhawk🎉Mythra─ ~/code/linux/rust-for-linux
 └$ git show 85eaa9c042c4
fatal: ambiguous argument '85eaa9c042c4': unknown revision or path not in the working tree.
Use '--' to separate paths from revisions, like this:
'git <command> [<revision>...] -- [<file>...]'

I guess I should clone your fork? I've only really used git like you would in github or gitlab, never with cross orgin repos.

git remote add wedsonaf git@github.com:wedsonaf/linux.git
git fetch wedsonaf

And then you can check out those commits

I see. Yeah, those commits are indeed in my fork.

Thanks Ariel!
no problem

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

Miguel suggested that we use ThinBox instead, but it needs some functions (e.g., from_raw/into_raw). The upside is that we could get O(1) behaviour storing just a pointer on the C side.

So basically we want to get rid of holding the vector we currently have and replace it with a basic pointer held by tinybox and then fetch the data from c whenever it needs to be accessed? Also how does this make it O(n) maybe im missing something from this code, like the underlying c code it calls or something.

The current solution doesn't use tinybox, so it needs to scan the string to determine its length (the string is nul-terminated), which makes it O(n) every time we want to convert back to CString or &CStr from the raw pointer

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

The current solution doesn't use tinybox, so it needs to scan the string to determine its length (the string is nul-terminated), which makes it O(n) every time we want to convert back to CString or &CStr from the raw pointer

Okay I understand what you want, is there a way I can test this? it just seems like im writting untestable code.

Not at all.

You can write tests that don't depend on kernel-specific functionality: for example, create a CString, call into_foreign() to turn it into a void, then call borrow() passing the void and check that what you get back is a proper &CStr, then call from_foreign to convert back to CString and check that you also get the proper string back. Perhaps do a couple of chained into_foreign/from_foreign to make sure you're not losing any state in the conversion.

The next step is then to design an ergonomic and zero-cost way to initialise such a CString from other buffers that aren't available all at once.

In any case, there's no problem if you feel like you need something simpler to begin with. Your questions make me think that may be the case.

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

Not at all.

You can write tests that don't depend on kernel-specific functionality: for example, create a CString, call into_foreign() to turn it into a void, then call borrow() passing the void and check that what you get back is a proper &CStr, then call from_foreign to convert back to CString and check that you also get the proper string back. Perhaps do a couple of chained into_foreign/from_foreign to make sure you're not losing any state in the conversion.

The next step is then to design an ergonomic and zero-cost way to initialise such a CString from other buffers that aren't available all at once.

In any case, there's no problem if you feel like you need something simpler to begin with. Your questions make me think that may be the case.

I actually have never done proper testing of any code before, I've done all manual testing so this is new to me. I believe so far I can come up with a zero-cost way for cstrings

In any case, there's no problem if you feel like you need something simpler to begin with. Your questions make me think that may be the case.

I did start this, but it turns out thinbox isn't in the kernel's alloc so I have to add that first.
I made a post in #Library about that

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

The next step is then to design an ergonomic and zero-cost way to initialise such a CString from other buffers that aren't available all at once.

In any case, there's no problem if you feel like you need something simpler to begin with. Your questions make me think that may be the case.

After researching and getting thinbox to work, I don't believe this is possible. At the low level unless you consume a CStr you can't just create a CString at o(n). Unless you want a shallow copy and for both of them to refer to the same data, but that seems wrong. Unless theres a way I don't see, this doesn't seem possible.
